content.full_text:
NAPA COUNTY                                    OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL

1195 THIRD STREET, ROOM 301 * NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94559-3001
AREA CODE 707/253-4521 FAX 707/253-4176

ROBERT WESTMEYER, County Counsel

December 13, 1991

MARGARET L. WOODBURY, Chief Deputy
JOSEPH C. FOLKARD, Deputy
DAVID L. ZALTSMAN, Deputy
KEVIN C. STOBAUGH, Deputy
JAYNE F. HERMAN, Deputy

Our File No. 1410.500


Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Attn: Legal Division

Ladies and Gentlemen:

Daniel M. Jonas, a commissioner on the Napa County Conservation, Development and
Planning Commission (the "Commission"), hereby requests a written opinion from
your office as to whether a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act
exists based upon the following facts:

Commissioner Jonas is President of, and holds one-third of the equity in, Triple
Delta Incorporated, a closely held corporation located at 730 California Blvd.,
Napa, CA 94559 ("Triple Delta"). Triple Delta regularly engages in the
construction of residential and commercial projects, both contractually and
speculatively. Neither Triple Delta nor Commissioner Jonas have owned or
developed any land in the unincorporated areas of Napa County during the past
twelve months nor does either plan, in the foreseeable future, to purchase or
develop any such land. Their future plans, however, could change should either
be presented with a favorable development opportunity.

In addition, Commissioner Jonas' wife, Janet E. Jonas, is currently employed as
a real estate agent for Coldwell Banker, Classical Properties, a real estate
firm located at 1750 First Street, Napa, CA 94559 ("Coldwell Banker"). As a
commissioned agent for Coldwell Banker, Ms. Jonas assists the firm in marketing
its listed properties including, from time to time, existing homes located in
unincorporated Napa County. Ms. Jonas currently has one single family home in
escrow in unincorporated Napa County, but has earned no commissions in the past
twelve months from the sale of property located in that area.

The Housing Element of the Napa County General Plan states that the provision of
affordable housing in the County is to be encouraged in connection with the
development of residential projects. The Commission is currently scheduled to
consider whether to recommend to the Napa County Board of Supervisors (the
"Board") that an inclusionary housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part
of a comprehensive response to this aspect of the County's General Plan. Such a
recommendation, if made, could include endorsement of a model ordinance

December 13, 1991
Page 2

requiring some or all of the following: that a certain percentage of all new
residential units constructed in a project be "affordable" as there is
defined in the ordinance; that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund in
lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or that construction of a
certain number of affordable units take place off-site. In addition, the
Commission is likely to recommend the creation of additional sources of funding
for affordable housing through means such as assessment of linkage fees from
developers of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon
project-generated employment, imposition of a business license tax or increases
in documentary transfer or sales taxes. Such taxes, if imposed or increased,
would be inapplicable to Commissioner Jonas' business and property interests
located within the City of Napa. Should Commissioner Jonas or Triple Delta ever
decide to commence a development project in unincorporated Napa County, such
project would be subject to any ordinance ultimately adopted by the Board.

The State Department of Housing and Community Development has required that the
Commission refer some form of inclusionary program to the Board by December 31,
1991. Failure to do so may reduce or eliminate the ability of the County to
qualify for Community Development Block Grant funds. We would therefore very
much appreciate expedited handling of this request and the return of a written
opinion to this office by December 26, 1991, if possible, so that a special
meeting of the Commission may be held on December 30, 1991. If you have any
questions as you conduct your review, please do not hesitate to contact either
me (707/253-4521) or Commissioner Jonas (707/255-8494 or 707/252-0554). Thank
you for your attention to this matter.

Very truly yours,

JAYNE F. HERMAN
Deputy County Counsel

cc:   Daniel M. Jonas
      Paul Battisti

[2000/request.opj]

Triple Delta regularly engages in the construction of residential and commercial projects, both contractually and speculatively. Neither Triple Delta nor Commissioner Jonas have owned or developed any land in the unincorporated areas of Napa County during the past twelve months nor does either plan, in the foreseeable future, to purchase or develop any such land. Their future plans, however, could change should either be presented with a favorable development opportunity. In addition, Commissioner Jonas' wife, Janet E. Jonas, is currently employed as a real estate agent for Coldwell Banker, Classical Properties, a real estate firm located at 1750 First Street, Napa, CA 94559 ("Coldwell Banker"). As a commissioned agent for Coldwell Banker, Ms. Jonas assists the firm in marketing its listed properties including, from time to time, existing homes located in unincorporated Napa County. Ms. Jonas currently has one single family home in escrow in unincorporated Napa County, but has earned no commissions in the past twelve months from the sale of property located in that area. The Housing Element of the Napa County General Plan states that the provision of affordable housing in the County is to be encouraged in connection with the development of residential projects. The Commission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recommend to the Napa County Board of Supervisors (the "Board") that an inclusionary housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a comprehensive response to this aspect of the County's General Plan. Such a recommendation, if made, could include endorsement of a model ordinance December 13, 1991 requiring some or all of the following: that a certain percentage of all new residential units constructed in a project be "affordable" as that term is defined in the ordinance; that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or that construction of a certain number of affordable units take place off-site. In addition, the Commission is likely to recommend the creation of additional sources of funding for affordable housing through means such as assessment of linkage fees from developers of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon project-generated employment, imposition of a business license tax or increases in documentary transfer or sales taxes. Such taxes, if imposed or increased, would be inapplicable to Commissioner Jonas' business and property interests located within the City of Napa.
